notes: Each rank adapts on 1 seq/chunk (vs 2 with 32K), same total gradient steps. More chunk boundaries means faster nll_scale reaction to local difficulty shifts. Rebased cleanly to exp_092 (removing discarded per-layer LR multipliers from exp_100).

hypothesis: Add learnable logit_bias [vocab_size=8192] zero-init vector, added inside the softcap before tanh, to give the model a dedicated unigram-prior channel disentangled from the tied embedding's semantic role (per "A Natural Bias for Language Generation Models", ACL 2023).
notes: logit_bias has numel=8192 <= 65536 so it passes through as float16 (16KB uncompressed, ~4-8KB brotli-compressed). Added to scalar_params (scalar_lr=0.02, adam_wd=0.02) so it gets full LR scheduling. Zero-init preserves step-0 identical to baseline. During TTT, it's in ttt_params (all parameters) and gets adapted per-chunk. Mechanism: tied embedding must simultaneously encode semantic similarity (for attention) and unigram frequency (for output). logit_bias separates these, letting the embedding focus on semantic content while logit_bias learns the per-token unigram prior. The ACL 2023 paper shows this improves learning efficiency and final performance by disentangling strong frequency effects.

hypothesis: Raise V/O WD to match MLP (muon_wd_vo 0.110→0.115) and restore Q/K WD to attn baseline (muon_wd_qk 0.075→0.095): V/O matrices perform content extraction like MLP and should share MLP-level regularization, while Q/K routing matrices keep baseline WD.
notes: 4-way WD split (from exp_105 structure): Q/K=0.095 (routing, baseline attn WD), V/O=0.115 (content, matches MLP), MLP=0.115. exp_105 tried Q/K=0.075/V/O=0.110 and discarded. This variant restores Q/K to baseline (exp_105 showed lower Q/K WD hurts) and raises V/O to MLP level (clean functional partition: routing vs content matrices).

hypothesis: Replace Nesterov-SGD TTT inner optimizer with Muon-style updates: NS5-orthogonalized gradient (using existing zeropower_via_newtonschulz5 in bfloat16 to reuse compiled graph) + Nesterov momentum buffer carried across chunks; LR scaled by 0.25× to match SGD update magnitude. Motivated by LaCT (arxiv 2505.23884) showing Muon consistently outperforms SGD momentum for chunk-wise TTT.
notes: NS5 on GPU for 512×512 matrices takes ~0.4μs each — negligible overhead. bfloat16 input to NS5 reuses the compiled graph from Muon training path. 0.25× LR scaling chosen so Muon update magnitude ≈ SGD update magnitude (NS5 normalizes gradient to unit spectral norm, so raw effective step is ~4× larger than SGD at same lr). Momentum buffers initialized as float32 for precision; convert to bf16 just before NS5. Carries momentum across chunk boundaries (sequential learning). Skips NS5 for 1D params (norms, gates) and tiny 2D params (q_gain [8,2]) — uses L2-normalized gradient there instead.

hypothesis: Three-phase batch schedule (quarter→half→full): add phase-0 with quarter-batch (196k tokens, 12 seqs/GPU) for the first 10% of training (0–60s), then phase-1 half-batch (28%) and full-batch (28–100%) as before; pre-compile the quarter-batch graph during warmup so no mid-training recompile overhead.
notes: CBS paper shows critical batch size starts near zero and grows rapidly early in training. Exp_109's 2-phase (half→full at 28%) yielded +0.0016 by concentrating more optimizer steps in the first 28% of training. Adding a quarter-batch phase-0 (0–10%) gives ~4x more optimizer steps vs full batch in the very first 60s when CBS is smallest. The additional warmup compilation pass is free from the 600s training budget since t0 starts after warmup. batch_phase0_tokens=196608=786432/4, batch_phase0_frac=0.10. | torch.distributed.elastic.multiprocessing.errors.ChildFailedError: ============================================================ (train_gpt.py:1170 in eval_val)

hypothesis: Lower embed_wd 0.085→0.05 on current best 092: exp_083 showed -0.000150 improvement over its parent (064) with this exact change but lost a sibling race to 082 by +0.000009 (pure noise); current best 092 inherits 082's embed_wd=0.085, so combining is genuinely untested. Mechanism (per arxiv 2603.26663) — tied tok_emb/lm_head behaves as output projection because output gradients dominate early; in our 600s undertrained regime WD=0.085 over-regularizes a still-converging output map. Independent of 082/092's TTT-side improvements.
notes: Untouched on current best lineage. exp_083 (parent=064) val_bpb=1.076547 vs 064's 1.076697 → consistent direction. Power Lines (2505.13738) + Weight Tying Biases (2603.26663) both predict lower WD optimum for tied embed in undertrained regime.

hypothesis: Depth-recurrence-aware q_gain: promote per-head bifurcated q_gain from [H,2] to [num_iters,H,2] so each of the 3 calls per loop layer (3,4,5) gets its own RoPE/NoPE channel scale, threaded as a Python int kwarg through forward (zero compute, ~352 fp32 params, step-0 numerically identical to current).
notes: Extends exp_030's bifurcation win (Δ=-0.000736) along the depth-recurrence axis. Loop layers 3-5 currently share one q_gain across iterations; this lets iter 0/1/2 differentiate (early=mixing, late=refinement). Uniform shape across all blocks for state-dict simplicity; non-loop blocks always use slot 0. iter_idx is Python int → torch.compile inlines as constant per call site (forward_logits for-loop is statically unrolled). q_gain matched by CONTROL_TENSOR_NAME_PATTERNS so fp32-restore still routes correctly with new ndim=3 shape; never quantized (not CastedLinear). Risk: extra slot-1/slot-2 only see gradient when looping_active=True (post enable_looping_at fraction); too few steps may leave them near init.

hypothesis: Reverse-position gentle CE weighting (1.1→0.9 linspace, mean=1.0): early sequence positions get 10% more gradient weight and late positions 10% less — the opposite direction from exp_057 (which tried 0.75→1.25) — to emphasise context-independent document-structure signal at sequence starts.
notes: Exp_057 tried up-weighting late positions (0.75→1.25) and hurt by +0.0015. The opposite direction (1.1→0.9, gentler at ±10%) has not been tried. Rationale: early-position predictions are harder (less context) but encode fundamental document-level statistics; giving them slightly more gradient may improve generalisation across all positions. All other loss modifications have failed; this is the one structural direction that remains unexplored.

hypothesis: Post-GPTQ Hessian-weighted optimal scale refinement: after SDClip GPTQ returns (Q, s_heuristic), recompute per-row scale as s_opt[r] = sum_j(H_jj * Q[r,j] * W[r,j]) / sum_j(H_jj * Q[r,j]^2), minimizing the Hessian-weighted reconstruction error for the given Q values. Zero artifact size impact since Q shape and s dtype/shape are unchanged.
notes: The GPTQ heuristic scale (clip_sigmas * row_std / clip_range) is set before error propagation and doesn't adapt to the final quantized Q values. The closed-form H-weighted optimal scale minimizes the actual reconstruction error for each row given the final Q. H is cloned inside gptq_quantize_weight so hessians[name] is intact for refinement. No size impact - same Q int8 tensor, same s float16 tensor shape.

hypothesis: Three-phase batch schedule (quarter→half→full) v2: phase-0 quarter-batch (196k tokens) for first 10% of training, phase-1 half-batch (393k) for 10–28%, phase-2 full-batch (786k) from 28%; quarter-batch graph compiles naturally at step 0 (all 8 GPUs simultaneously, no NCCL risk) removing the 4th warmup pass that caused v1 crash.
notes: v1 (exp_115) crashed at eval_val line 1170 with total_s=960s, indicating the extra warmup compilation pass (~60s overhead) was destabilizing eval. Fix: remove phase0 warmup pass entirely; instead the quarter-batch shape compiles at training step 0 when all 8 DDP ranks arrive simultaneously (safe). Half-batch and full-batch shapes are still pre-compiled in warmup as before. | torch.distributed.elastic.multiprocessing.errors.ChildFailedError: ============================================================ (train_gpt.py:1170 in eval_val)

hypothesis: Learnable logit_bias with 512 frequency buckets (vocab_size//16, zero-init), expanded via repeat_interleave(16) and added inside the softcap — gives a coarse unigram-prior channel disentangled from the tied embedding, while fitting within the artifact budget (~875 compressed bytes vs 14KB for the full-vocab version).
notes: exp_112 (same idea, full vocab_size=8192) was size_blocked by 6,715 bytes — the 16KB float16 passthrough compressed to ~14KB. Reducing to 512 buckets (16 tokens/bucket, consecutive token IDs) shrinks the float16 passthrough to 1024 bytes (~875 bytes compressed), saving ~13KB and leaving ~6KB headroom. Bucket resolution is coarse but consecutive BPE token IDs have rough frequency correlation (lower IDs tend to be more common). The 512-level frequency prior still disentangles high-frequency from low-frequency token groups, allowing the tied embedding to focus on semantic similarity.

hypothesis: Raise AdamW β₂ from 0.95→0.99 at warmdown start for tok_emb and scalar optimizer groups: stable second-moment estimates let the LR schedule—not gradient magnitude fluctuations—control step size during warmdown (arxiv 2508.01483: consistent improvements with higher β₂ during WSD cooldown).
notes: Abrupt switch: when elapsed_frac >= 1-warmdown_frac (i.e. t>=168s), set beta2=0.99 for optimizer_tok and optimizer_scalar. Muon unchanged (first-moment-only, no beta2). Only activates once per training run via the _g['betas'][1] != h.beta2_warmdown guard. beta2_warmdown=0.99 vs base beta2=0.95.

hypothesis: Fix eval_budget_overrun from exp_114 by replacing compiled zeropower_via_newtonschulz5 with non-compiled inline _ttt_ns5 (3 steps, eager) in the TTT Muon update, eliminating torch.compile recompilation overhead (~8-10s) that caused the 3.6s budget overrun.
notes: exp_114 eval_budget_overrun at 603.6s (only 3.6s over). Root cause: @torch.compile on zeropower_via_newtonschulz5 triggered recompilation when called from TTT path (different call site from training Muon). Raw NS5 computation for all matrices across all TTT steps is ~2-4s total (negligible on GPU). Non-compiled eager version avoids all recompilation overhead; 3 steps instead of 5 is a further safety margin. Core hypothesis unchanged: Muon-style orthogonalized gradient updates (per LaCT arxiv 2505.23884) should outperform Nesterov SGD for chunk-wise TTT.

hypothesis: Give skip_weights and skip_gates their own AdamW param group with weight_decay=0.0 (freed from adam_wd=0.02): WD was decaying these critical skip-connection parameters by ~80% over training (scalar_lr×adam_wd per step over ~4000 steps ≈ e^-1.6 factor), forcing the optimizer to constantly fight WD just to maintain useful skip magnitudes.
notes: exp_067 appeared to try WD=0 for skip params but actually kept skip_weight in the adam_wd=0.02 group (the WD=0 group was for 'pure norm/bias params' which don't exist in this model - RMSNorm has no learnable params). This is genuinely the first experiment giving skip_weights/skip_gates WD=0. skip_weights initialized at ones(num_skip_weights, dim), WD=0.02 was pulling them toward zero, gradient had to compensate. Without WD tension the skip connections should converge faster to their optimal magnitudes.

hypothesis: Lower muon_momentum_warmup_start 0.92 to 0.85 (canonical Modded-NanoGPT value): untouched in 116 trials despite peak and fraction being tuned; lower start means first ~10% of training has Muon updates 50% closer to on-policy gradient (1-0.85=0.15 vs 1-0.92=0.08), giving better basin selection in the highly-plastic 600s undertrained regime, then ramps to preserved peak 0.99 over the same 0.33 fraction.
notes: Untouched axis verified via tree.tsv grep across 116 trials. Peak (muon_momentum=0.99) tested via exp_044 lowering=discard, so peak stays. Ramp fraction tested via exp_064 keep (0.45) but reverted at exp_082; exp_093 retried=discard, so fraction stays at 0.33. Start value is the only un-swept knob. Modded-NanoGPT canonical (Keller Jordan, Muon-LLM arxiv 2502.16982) uses start=0.85 with peak=0.95; we keep our high peak but adopt canonical start. Mechanism: momentum=0.85 gives ~6.7-step effective averaging at start vs ~12.5 at 0.92, closer to single-step on-policy gradient when loss landscape is most plastic. Single-line change.

hypothesis: Extend half-batch phase from 28% to 38% of training: keep 2-phase schedule (half→full) but with transition at 38% instead of 28%, giving 10% more of training (60s) with more optimizer steps while LR is still at 88% of peak.
notes: CBS theory (arxiv 2505.23971) and Fast Catch-Up Late Switching (2602.14208) suggest optimal batch size grows monotonically through training. Exp_109 switches at frac=0.28 (warmdown start). At frac=0.38, LR is still 88% of peak, so half-batch (more optimizer steps at near-peak LR) is still beneficial. Removed quarter-batch phase (batch_phase0_frac=0.0) to avoid 3-shape compiled graph issues that caused crashes in exp_115/120. Phase-0 logic is present but dormant. | torch.distributed.elastic.multiprocessing.errors.ChildFailedError: ============================================================ (train_gpt.py:1170 in eval_val)

hypothesis: Add PaLM-style z-loss (1e-4 * mean(logsumexp(logits)^2)) to the CE loss: penalizes the log-partition function magnitude, keeping logit scale well-conditioned and reducing the effective weight singular-value range needed — improving GPTQ int6 quantization fidelity.
notes: z-loss from PaLM (Chowdhery et al. 2022). With logit_softcap=30, logsumexp is bounded ~[0,39]; z_loss adds at most ~0.15 nats early in training, settling to ~0.01 nats in steady state. The gradient pushes weight matrices toward lower spectral norm, which is exactly what GPTQ benefits from (less quantization error when weights don't need large singular values to produce confident logits). Rebased from exp_109; no position weighting residue.

hypothesis: Switch MLP activation from LeakyReLU(slope=0.5).square() to ReLU.square() (PrimerEZ / Haziza 2025 srelu): zero the negative tail entirely instead of leaking 0.25·z², inducing activation sparsity that may regularize the undertrained MLP. One-character edit (negative_slope 0.5→0.0 equivalent); identical shapes / dtypes / compile-graph topology / param count, so eval budget is unaffected.
notes: Genuinely untried in our tree — no arch trial has touched the activation. Loss case (Δ ≥ 0) is informative because it confirms the LeakyReLU(0.5) slope was deliberately tuned for grad-flow in our undertrained regime; win case validates that PrimerEZ's sparsity claim transfers to small-LM scale. Lowest-risk arch edit available given saturation + recent eval_budget_overrun pattern from any param/graph change (exp_106 Peri-LN, exp_117 q_gain[num_iters,H,2], exp_088 SWA all overran from compile-side cost). Sources: arxiv 2109.08668 (Primer), Haziza/FAIR 2025 srelu sparsity post.

hypothesis: Tighten TTT gradient clip from 1.0 to 0.5: motivated by the LaCT paper (arXiv:2505.23884) finding that sequential gradient accumulation in TTT causes magnitude explosion; halving the clip norm reduces per-step parameter drift, preventing overfitting to each chunk while matching the tighter 0.3 clip used during training.
notes: Training clip is 0.3, TTT was 1.0 (3.3x looser). Nesterov momentum=0.9 carries velocity across 500+ chunks. Tighter clip reduces accumulation. Novel to tree (no prior TTT grad clip experiment). LaCT paper 2025 specifically identifies gradient explosion as the core TTT stability problem.

hypothesis: Loss-weighted GPTQ Hessian calibration: two-pass approach computes per-batch CE loss then re-accumulates Hessian with each batch weighted by its normalized loss, biasing GPTQ error compensation toward sequences where the model is most uncertain (highest BPB contribution).
notes: Two-pass: pass-1 runs n_calibration_batches forward passes to get per-batch CE loss (no hooks), pass-2 re-forwards stored batches with Hessian accumulation multiplied by alpha=norm_loss_weight (hooks use addmm_ with alpha=batch_weight[0] mutable container). Weights normalized to sum to n_calibration_batches so the final /n_calibration_batches normalization stays compatible. Zero model size impact. ~2x forward-pass cost for Hessian collection (additional ~5-10s, within eval budget).

hypothesis: Add learnable per-feature input embedding scale [model_dim=512], ones-init, applied after functional RMSNorm on token embeddings; decouples per-dimension input sensitivity from the tied output projection and lets the model emphasize useful embedding directions without adding vocab-sized params.
notes: Current functional F.rms_norm(x,(x.size(-1),)) has no learnable weights. Adding embed_scale=[512] initialized to ones gives the model per-feature amplification/suppression at the input stage. With tied embeddings (same matrix for input+output), input and output use the embedding vectors in different roles; a scale separates these. embed_scale is added to scalar_params group (AdamW scalar_lr=0.02, adam_wd=0.02). Cost: ~2KB float32, compresses to ~100 bytes (all-ones). Previous tok experiments: bigram injection (exp_102, discard) and logit_bias/buckets (exp_112/121, discard/size_blocked). This is a different structural change targeting input normalization.

hypothesis: Delay LR warmdown start from 28% to 35% (warmdown_frac 0.72→0.65): with exp_109's batch curriculum, batch switches from half→full at exactly t=28%=warmdown start, so the model never sees full-batch training at peak LR; separating the transitions by 7% (~42s) lets the optimizer exploit stable full-batch gradients before entering convergence mode.
notes: warmdown_frac 0.72→0.65. Area-under-LR-curve increases from 0.648 to 0.682 (+5%). The batch switch (batch_phase1_frac=0.28) and warmdown start (1-warmdown_frac) currently coincide at t=28%; the fast-catch-up effect (arxiv 2602.14208) suggests the model needs peak-LR time with the larger batch to adapt before LR decay begins. min_lr floor phase length stays nearly the same (~9.75% vs 10.8%).

hypothesis: Restore exp_092's validated TTT improvements onto exp_109: add nesterov=True to TTT SGD and recurrence-block gradient equalization (rescale blocks 3-5 grad by 1/(num_loops+1)=1/3 after backward), which were lost when the curr specialist built exp_109 from exp_092's snapshot without carrying forward these TTT gains.
notes: exp_082→exp_092 (on similar baseline without batch scheduling) gained Δ=-0.000141 from exactly these two changes. exp_109 uses plain SGD(momentum=0.9) with no gradient equalization. Adding nesterov=True corrects the lookahead step; equalization fixes the 3× gradient accumulation bias in loop blocks 3-5 (num_loops=2 → scale=1/3). No change to model arch or size — pure TTT improvement. These are the same code patterns from the ttt workdir's exp_092 keepfile.

hypothesis: Extend half-batch phase from 28% to 34% of training: transition stays below enable_looping_at=0.35 so looping always activates with the pre-compiled full-batch shape, avoiding the uncompiled [half-batch+looping] shape that caused exp_126's crash.
notes: Root bug in exp_115/120/126: batch_phase1_frac > enable_looping_at (0.35) caused half-batch+looping to be used at frac=0.35 — an uncompiled shape not pre-warmed in warmup. Fix: batch_phase1_frac=0.34 < 0.35, so looping activates only after full-batch is active (pre-compiled). Extends the half-batch optimizer-step advantage by 6% of training (~36 more seconds of 2x update density). LR at frac=0.34 is still 91% of peak.

hypothesis: Lower adam_eps 1e-8→1e-10: untouched in 127 trials. With tied tok_emb (vocab=8192), per-row gradients for moderately-rare tokens are ~1e-5 to 1e-4, so v~1e-10 to 1e-8 is comparable to eps=1e-8 — the eps floor suppresses Adam's adaptive normalization on long-tail vocab rows. Lowering to 1e-10 lets sqrt(v) dominate even for rows with grad above ~1e-6, restoring per-row effective LR for the rare-token rows that disproportionately impact BPB.
notes: Per Zack Nado's "ε, A Nuisance No More": smaller eps preserves useful low-curvature directions; "exponential decay schedule from initial values to near-zero is recommended". Affects both AdamW groups (optimizer_tok and optimizer_scalar share h.adam_eps). Scalar group impact is minimal (grads O(1), v dominates eps already). The mechanism is concentrated on tok_emb rare-token rows. Conservative magnitude (4 orders down from 1e-8 to 1e-10, not 1e-15) to avoid numerical instability while clearing the eps-bottleneck regime for moderately rare tokens.

hypothesis: Token-level data augmentation: randomly replace 1% of input tokens with uniform-random vocab tokens during training, forcing the model to use broader context rather than relying on immediate-predecessor copying, potentially improving robustness to small perturbations analogous to GPTQ quantization noise.
notes: Novel in this run: stochastic depth (exp_036, +0.009), MLP dropout (exp_095, +0.005), label smoothing (exp_056, +0.045) all hurt. WD tweaks exhausted. Token masking is applied only to x (input), not y (target), so the supervision signal is unaffected. Disabled at eval/TTT/quantization (those code paths don't go through step_fn). Rate=0.01 means ~20 tokens corrupted per 2048-token sequence.

hypothesis: Disable XSA on parallel-residual decoder layers (7-10): XSA's self-V-projection subtraction is an inductive bias against degenerate attention-output redundancy in the *sequential* pre-norm residual stream; in the GPT-J-style parallel-residual topology (layers 7-10) attn and MLP both write into x_in independently rather than chained, so XSA's residual-stream rationale is absent. Compute-negative (saves 4 normalize+reduction passes/forward → buys eval-budget headroom against the 600s cap that the prior 5 arch trials kept overrunning); step-0 bit-identical because proj._zero_init=True forces attn_out=0.
notes: Single-loop-bound edit in GPT.__init__ XSA enablement: range cap from h.num_layers to h.parallel_residual_start (=7). Pure config change; no graph-topology mutation, no new params, no kernel swap. compute-NEGATIVE (4 fewer XSA calls per fwd). Specifically targets the eval_budget_overrun pattern (exp_106/097/088/117/128 all overran). XSA stays on layers 0..6 (encoder + 3-layer recurrence), where the looped traversal (3× per forward of layers 3-5) is exactly where the XSA self-subtraction inductive bias is most useful (prevents redundant self-correction across loop iterations). Layers 7-10 (parallel-residual) free to use full self-attention.

hypothesis: Reset SGD momentum buffer between TTT chunks: FineWeb validation spans diverse topics across consecutive 32K-token chunks, so stale inter-chunk velocity from chunk k misguides the first few gradient steps of chunk k+1; zeroing the buffer before each chunk preserves within-chunk momentum accumulation while eliminating cross-chunk contamination.
notes: Zero-cost change (just zeros momentum_buffer tensors). The SGD optimizer is constructed once and Nesterov momentum accumulates across all ~500 chunks; with ttt_epochs=2 and ~4 sequences/rank, each chunk runs ~8 gradient steps, so at the end of a chunk the velocity magnitude ≈ 1/(1-0.9)=10 steps of accumulated gradient. This stale velocity from potentially unrelated content bleeds into chunk k+1 early steps. Resetting removes inter-chunk interference without affecting eval wall time.

hypothesis: BPE-rank frequency-stratified loss weighting: up-weight rare tokens (higher vocab IDs) by up to 15% using normalized BPE rank as a frequency proxy (weight = 1 + 0.15 * rank, mean=1), compensating for their systematic underrepresentation in training batches vs their equal per-occurrence contribution to BPB.
notes: BPE vocab sorts tokens by merge frequency: low IDs = common, high IDs = rare. Rare tokens appear less per batch but contribute equally to BPB. Gentle up-weighting (α=0.15, max ratio 1.15) keeps the modification mild enough to not hurt common-token learning while giving rare tokens slightly more gradient signal. Contrast with exp_040 (byte-weighted TTT, discard) which was eval-only; this is a training-time modification using token-ID rank rather than actual byte counts.

hypothesis: Combined frequency+loss Hessian weighting: extend exp_130's loss-weighted calibration by also scaling activations for the top-100 most-frequent tokens by sqrt(2), so the Hessian simultaneously emphasizes high-uncertainty batches (loss weight) and frequent tokens (freq weight), two complementary orthogonal signals for GPTQ error compensation.
notes: Exp_002 showed freq-only weighting gave -1.8 mBPB on baseline but +0.54 mBPB regression on later lineage (exp_081). Exp_130 showed loss-only weighting gave -0.05 mBPB. The combination applies batch-level loss weight AND token-level freq^2 weight in H=sum(w_batch * (x*fw)^T(x*fw)). The top-100 frequent token activations get 2x Hessian contribution (sqrt(2)^2). This is novel — neither tried on current best lineage. Size risk is low since it only changes calibration weighting, not scale distributions.

hypothesis: Add within-word BPE position embeddings: zero-init nn.Embedding(8, 512) injected after input RMSNorm using a causal cummax scan over SP8192 word-start flags (persistent=False LUT, not in artifact), giving the model explicit intra-word token position without future leakage.
notes: The SP8192 tokenizer splits words into BPE subwords; position-within-word (0=first subword, 1=second, ...) is causal information the model must currently re-learn from attention patterns. The cummax trick over word-start flags (tokens with leading '▁') computes within-word position in O(T) with no loops. LUT is persistent=False so only 8x512=4096 float16 params (~4KB brotli-compressed) go into the artifact. word_pos_emb.weight trained with tied_embed_lr=0.03 via AdamW alongside tok_emb.

hypothesis: Raise tied_embed_lr 0.03→0.04 (an axis untouched in 135 trials): with tied embeddings the tok_emb param receives output-projection (softmax-side) gradients which dominate the input-embedding gradients — currently the tied_embed_lr/matrix_lr ratio of 1.36 is closer to μP-style than to the √d≈22.6 ratio recommended by arxiv 2506.15025 for large-vocab models, so a +33% bump samples the theoretically-favored direction while staying small-radius (still 15× below the untied default of 0.6).
notes: Untouched axis. exp_122 (opt) showed tok_emb group is sensitive to β2 raises (variance-smoothing change, lost), but a peak-LR change is mechanistically different. With min_lr=0.15 floor and 72% warmdown, late-training updates remain substantial, so peak embed LR has real leverage. If win, follow up with 0.045/0.05; if loss, direction is "lower" (try 0.025/0.02).

hypothesis: Implement Muon-VS (Variance-Adaptive Muon, arxiv 2601.14603): add per-element variance scaling of the Nesterov momentum before Newton-Schulz orthogonalization using the existing muon_beta2=0.95 EMA buffer, dampening high-variance gradient directions and amplifying low-variance ones for more efficient orthogonal updates.
notes: Muon-VS from arxiv 2601.14603 (Jan 2026) shows 1.36x speedup on LLaMA-1.2B for same compute. Mechanism: before orthogonalization, element-wise scale gradient by 1/sqrt(running_var) — equivalent to Adam's adaptive step in the Muon direction space. Uses existing unused muon_beta2=0.95 hyperparameter. Applied after Nesterov momentum, before row_normalize and Newton-Schulz. var_buf stored in float32 for precision. Changes: (1) Muon.__init__ accepts muon_beta2, (2) Muon.step() maintains var_buf + vs_step per parameter and applies variance scaling, (3) Optimizers passes h.muon_beta2.

hypothesis: Hard-token loss weighting: replace uniform CE mean with per-token loss-proportional weights (w = per_tok / mean_per_tok, detached), computing mean(per_tok²)/mean(per_tok) — upweights hard tokens ~proportionally to their difficulty, concentrating gradient on positions where the model errs most.
notes: α=2 focal-style weighting without extra hyperparameter. For median loss ≈3 nats, easy tokens (loss=0.5) get weight 0.17x, hard tokens (loss=4.0) get 1.33x — ~8x differential. Fully fullgraph-compatible (all fixed-shape ops, detach stops gradient through weights). Only changes the training forward path; eval/TTT code uses its own cross_entropy calls unchanged.

hypothesis: Fuse per-parameter TTT gradient AllReduce (150+ individual NCCL calls) into a single float32 flat-buffer AllReduce per step to eliminate kernel-launch overhead (~15ms/step → ~14s saved), then use the freed eval budget to add a 4th TTT epoch (ttt_epochs 3→4, +33% adaptation per chunk at ~1ms cost).
notes: Two compound changes: (1) Replace the per-parameter `for p in ttt_params: dist.all_reduce(p.grad)` loop with a pre-allocated float32 flat buffer + single dist.all_reduce, reducing NCCL launch overhead from ~150 launches/step to 1 launch/step. On 8 GPUs NVLink, each NCCL launch has ~50-100us overhead; 150 launches x 3 steps x 305 chunks = ~14-28s saved. (2) Use that freed budget to run 4 TTT epochs instead of 3; the extra epoch gives the Nesterov SGD another pass over each chunk's 4 sequences per rank. These synergize: fused allreduce makes each training step cheaper, allowing the extra epoch without overrun risk.

hypothesis: Add per-head sigmoid output gate on attention (NeurIPS 2025 Best Paper, arXiv:2505.06708): apply gate=sigmoid(α·||y||₂ + β) per head with α=0/β=+4 init (transparent at step-0) between SDPA(+XSA) and proj, introducing non-linearity on the low-rank softmax mapping plus output-magnitude-dependent sparse gating to mitigate attention-sink at near-zero param/byte cost (176 scalars total).
notes: Direct W_g[dim,H] linear port would add ~45k params (~50 KB compressed), too tight under our 16 MB cap. Cheap-adaptation derives the gate from per-head L2 norm of the SDPA output via two learnable per-head scalars α,β (8+8 per layer × 11 = 176 scalars). β=+4 → sigmoid(4)=0.982 at init, so step-0 is numerically near-transparent (proj is zero-init anyway). Once proj learns, α can drift signed: positive α gives "high-norm heads pass through, low-norm muted" sparse gating; negative α gives the inverse; both are richer than the existing per-feature attn_scale (linear, no input dependence). ndim=1 → routed to scalar AdamW group via p.ndim<2 check; no CONTROL_TENSOR_NAME_PATTERNS update needed. Gate placed AFTER XSA (paper's G1 position), before reshape+proj. Recent arch trials hit eval_budget_overruns (exp_117, exp_128) — this edit adds only one elementwise sigmoid per attn block, so eval cost should be flat. Risk: y.float().norm(...) may be slightly slower than I estimate; if eval overruns, try y.norm(dim=-1) in bf16 directly.

hypothesis: Set scalar_wd=0.0 (down from adam_wd=0.02) for optimizer_scalar: attn_scale, mlp_scale, resid_mix, and q_gain are initialized at non-zero target values (1.0, 1.0, [1.0,0], 5.25) yet WD constantly pulls them toward zero, creating optimization friction that forces gradients to fight the WD force rather than learning the optimal scale.
notes: Key control tensors (q_gain init=5.25, attn_scale/mlp_scale init=1, resid_mix[0] init=1) are all in optimizer_scalar with WD=0.02. At scalar_lr=0.02 over 3000 steps, WD shrinks q_gain from 5.25 to ~1.6 absent compensating gradients — a 70% pull toward zero. Removing WD from scalars eliminates this constant tension, letting the optimizer settle at gradient-determined equilibria for scale/gate params without fighting decay. exp_124 tried a separate WD group for skip_weights/skip_gates only; this goes further to cover all control tensors.

hypothesis: Replace cumulative NLL running-average reference in TTT adaptive LR with EMA (decay=0.95, α=0.05): compares each chunk's difficulty to a ~20-chunk local window instead of all prior chunks, making the cosine LR scaling more responsive to FineWeb's domain-shift structure.
notes: exp_082 established that chunk_nll/running_avg_nll LR scaling helps (-0.000045). Cumulative average dilutes recent domain-shift signal at late chunks (chunk 300 compares to average of all 300). EMA (decay=0.95, half-life ~14 chunks) focuses on recent ~450K tokens, reacting faster to difficulty spikes from topic transitions. Update order: compute nll_scale from prior EMA, then update EMA. First chunk initializes EMA=chunk_nll_0, nll_scale=1.0 (same as before). Code is otherwise identical to exp_130 (rebased from best snapshot, no momentum-reset code).

hypothesis: Reduce GPTQ block_size 128→64 and damp_factor 0.01→0.005: finer inter-block error propagation plus less Hessian over-regularization should improve quantization fidelity without changing artifact size.
notes: block_size 128→64 doubles global error-propagation frequency (most impactful for wide proj matrices cols=2048: 16→32 blocks). damp_factor 0.01→0.005 halves Hessian regularization, giving more precise inverse. Both are independent improvements that compound. The gptq_quantize_weight default was 128/0.01; neither was ever explicitly set as a hyperparameter in our lineage, so this is first exploration of this axis.

hypothesis: MiLe Loss (arXiv 2310.19531): replace static BPE-rank weighting with dynamic entropy-weighted CE — weight_i = sqrt(1 + H(p_i)) / mean, where H(p_i) is Shannon entropy of model's prediction at each position; early training is near-uniform (undertrained model → high entropy everywhere), warmdown increasingly down-weights confident easy tokens and focuses gradient on remaining hard tokens.
notes: MiLe Loss formula: weight = (1+H)^0.5 / mean[(1+H)^0.5] where H = Shannon entropy in nats. H computed via log_softmax under torch.no_grad() so only affects loss weights not backprop graph. Key difference from exp_139 BPE-rank weighting: MiLe weights are dynamic (change as model learns), providing a natural curriculum — standard CE early, focused on hard tokens late. Replaces exp_139's discarded static BPE-rank weighting. Based on MiLe Loss paper showing 0.1% perplexity improvement on large models.

hypothesis: Raise ttt_momentum 0.9→0.93 (untouched scalar axis in 142 trials): three prior trials (exp_031, exp_091, exp_138) all reset the cross-chunk SGD momentum buffer and all regressed +0.0005-0.0009, establishing that cross-chunk velocity carryover is beneficial; raising β to 0.93 extends velocity timescale 10→14 inner steps, amplifying the same beneficial smoothing without zeroing it.
notes: Pure scalar change (zero size/time impact). With nesterov=True and TTT_LR=0.005, effective steady-state step grows 9×LR→13.3×LR (peak ~0.067), well within stable range. The Marginal Value of Momentum (arxiv 2307.15196) shows momentum value matters most in noisy small-LR regimes — exactly TTT's regime. Direction (up not down) is chosen because the three reset-the-buffer ablations all argue that more carryover, not less, is what helps.

hypothesis: Add per-dimension SmearAlpha: zero-init [model_dim=512] parameter that additively injects the previous position's normalized embedding into the current embedding (`x += smear_alpha * x_prev`), giving the model cheap causal bigram context in the input layer at zero initial cost (identity at init, ≤200 compressed bytes).
notes: SmearGate concept from PR #462 (achieved 1.0672 in a different stack). Additive formulation with zero-init is strictly identity at step 0 — safer than PR #462's sigmoid(0)=0.5 initialization. Placed after input RMSNorm so baseline scale is preserved. Optimizer: scalar_params (scalar_lr=0.02). TTT adapts smear_alpha per-chunk. Size impact: 512 float16 passthrough ≈ 200 compressed bytes, well within headroom.

hypothesis: Apply gradient clipping only to AdamW param groups (embed+scalar) instead of all parameters: Muon's Newton-Schulz orthogonalization already normalizes update matrices to unit spectral norm, so the global pre-NS clip was distorting gradient directions entering the momentum buffer; removing the clip for Muon lets NS capture the true gradient distribution.
notes: The global clip_grad_norm_ was applied to all params before the Muon step, meaning the clipped gradients entered the Muon momentum buffer. NS then normalizes to unit spectral norm regardless, so the clip only hurt by distorting the momentum direction. AdamW groups (embed_wd, scalar_wd) genuinely need the clip for stability. This edit separates them: Muon gets raw gradients, AdamW gets clipped at 0.3.

hypothesis: Fuse per-parameter TTT gradient AllReduce (150+ NCCL calls) into a single float32 flat-buffer AllReduce per step; revert ttt_epochs to 3 after exp_145 showed 4 epochs overruns budget by ~3.5s.
notes: Exp_145 confirmed 4 epochs causes eval_budget_overrun. This submit keeps only the fused AllReduce: (1) eliminates 150+ NCCL launches per gradient step → saves NCCL overhead and reduces eval time headroom; (2) AllReduce is done in float32 (vs current BF16 per-tensor), giving more precise gradient averaging across 8 GPUs. Gradient quality improvement from float32 averaging is the only quality mechanism here. Expected delta is small but the approach is budget-safe.

hypothesis: Add mid-warmdown shard reshuffle at frac=0.55: the 72%-long warmdown runs on a single fixed shard permutation set at frac=0.28; a second reshuffle midway through (frac=0.55, LR still at 62% of peak) gives fresh data orderings for the second half of warmdown, improving data diversity during convergence.
notes: Warmdown covers 72% of training (432s on 8 GPUs). Shards hold ~500M tokens each; only ~25% consumed per shard during warmdown, so no auto-reshuffle occurs. Adding a reshuffle at the 55% time mark (midpoint between warmdown start at 28% and the last ~85% where LR hits floor) exposes the model to fresh shard starting positions in the second half of warmdown. Implementation: 4 lines added to training loop, no batch/shape changes, no compile issues.

hypothesis: Bifurcated K-gain [num_kv_heads=4, 2] mirroring exp_030's Q-gain win on the K side: split K into RoPE/NoPE channels and apply per-kv-head learnable scale (init=1.0, step-0 identical) so each kv-head can independently sharpen the RoPE vs NoPE contribution to logits — a DOF that q_gain[H,2] cannot express because Q-side scaling is finer-grained per-q-head but identical across the 2 q-heads sharing each kv-head.
notes: Structural completion of the exp_030 Q-gain bifurcation (-0.000736 keep). Adds 8 fp32 scalars/layer × 11 = 88 total = 352 B (passthrough float16 in GPTQ, well under the 65536 numel cutoff). Routed to scalar AdamW group via CONTROL_TENSOR_NAME_PATTERNS += k_gain, mirroring q_gain. Step-0 numerically identical (init=1.0, plus proj zero-init makes attn_out=0 anyway). Compute: one extra elementwise multiply + cat per attn forward (negligible vs flash_attn_3). Risk: tiny.

hypothesis: Lower embed_wd from 0.085 to 0.05: in the severely undertrained 10-minute regime with tiny embed init (std=0.005) and low tied_embed_lr=0.03, the current WD over-constrains the embedding, preventing it from learning distinctive token representations; reducing WD by ~41% lets embeddings grow more expressive while logit_softcap=30.0 prevents overconfidence.
notes: embed_wd is the only major WD axis untouched across 147 experiments. All other reg attempts (dropout, stochastic depth, augmentation, WD splits within matrix types) have been tried. embed_wd=0.085 is below attn WD=0.095 but the effective per-step decay at tied_embed_lr=0.03 is already gentler than matrix LR=0.022 (wait, actually matrix_lr=0.022 which is LOWER than tied_embed_lr=0.03). The embedding receives large output-projection gradients through tied weights, potentially making WD-driven shrinkage counterproductive in this data-constrained regime.

hypothesis: Add floor of max(0.3, ...) to cosine TTT LR decay: prevents near-zero adaptation on late validation chunks where domain shift may demand more gradient signal, consistent with 'LR decay wastes challenging late data' findings (arxiv 2511.18903).
notes: Current cosine gives last 37% of TTT chunks less than 30% of peak LR (last chunk: 0% LR). With nll_scale clamped 0.5-2.0, a difficult late chunk that signals nll_scale=2.0 still only gets ~0.1*ttt_lr instead of ~0.6*ttt_lr. Floor at 0.3 ensures late hard chunks get meaningful adaptation. Zero eval wall-time impact (same epoch count, just different LR values).

hypothesis: Upgrade GPTQ Hessian calibration from per-batch loss weighting (exp_130) to per-token loss weighting: each token position's activation is scaled by sqrt(loss_t/mean_loss) so H = sum_t (loss_t/mean_loss)*x_t*x_t^T, concentrating calibration on positions where quantization error most degrades BPB.
notes: Per-token weighting is strictly finer than per-batch (exp_130). Within a batch, easy tokens (function words, repetitive patterns) have low CE loss and get low Hessian weight; hard tokens (rare words, long-range dependencies) get up-weighted. This should reduce quantization error exactly at the positions contributing most to val_bpb. Implementation: two-pass calibration; first pass collects per-token CE loss via reduction='none', second pass scales activations by sqrt(loss_t/mean_loss) before Hessian accumulation. No change to model size, block_size=128, damp=0.01 unchanged from exp_130.

hypothesis: Raise tied_embed_init_std 0.005→0.020: an untouched meta axis in 151 trials. Tied tok_emb doubles as output projection so init logit std = σ·√d = σ·22.6; current 0.005 → 0.113 (near-uniform softmax for hundreds of steps wasting CE-grad signal); 0.020 → 0.45 (meaningfully non-uniform softmax from step 1, well below logit_softcap=30 so no saturation), giving downstream layers informative gradients earlier in the 600s undertrained regime.
notes: Pure init change, zero size/time impact. σ=0.02 is the canonical Karpathy/GPT-2/nanoGPT transformer init for both input and output embeddings; current 0.005 is 4× smaller without obvious justification (likely a relic from an earlier untied-lm_head config where small input-only init made sense). With logit_softcap=30 the softcap is effectively identity at init logits ~0.45 (tanh(0.015)≈identity), so no saturation risk. Mechanism: with σ=0.005 the model spends the first ~100-300 steps doing essentially uniform-softmax forward passes that produce diffuse CE gradients before any structure emerges; σ=0.02 jumpstarts that structure, compounding through every subsequent step in our compute-limited regime. Risk axis: instability if init too large — but at 0.02 we're still 100× below FCN-style 1/sqrt(d)≈0.044 init scale and well within typical transformer practice.

hypothesis: Token Order Prediction (TOP, arXiv 2508.19228) auxiliary pairwise hinge loss: at each position i, apply α=0.1·mean(relu(logit[t+2]−logit[t+1])) so the existing logit at position i is penalized when it ranks the 2-ahead token above the immediate next token; replaces discarded MiLe Loss with a sparse ranking constraint that uses zero new parameters and is fullgraph=True compatible.
notes: exp_080 tried CE at offset 2 (α=0.15) → catastrophic +0.043; TOP hinge is fundamentally different: it only fires (sparse gradient) when the ordering is wrong, creating mild regularization rather than conflicting distribution targets. fullgraph=True safe: all tensor ops, no Python conditionals. TOP paper (arXiv 2508.19228) reports consistent gains over NTP at 340M–7B; this is the zero-param analogue using the tied-embedding logit with a hinge instead of CE objective.

hypothesis: Double TTT chunk size 32768→65536: same total training FLOPs (halved chunk count × doubled seqs/chunk) but 2× larger effective batch per optimizer step, giving lower gradient variance and halving AllReduce overhead (~210→105 total calls).
notes: Doubling ttt_chunk_tokens from 32768 to 65536. Total training work per GPU unchanged: 70×3×8=1680 sequences (32K) vs 35×3×16=1680 sequences (64K). Each optimizer step now averages gradient over 128 sequences (8 GPUs × 16 each) instead of 64, reducing gradient noise by ~√2. Also halves total AllReduce calls from ~210 to ~105, freeing eval budget. The cosine LR decay over fewer chunks means the LR stays higher for longer, which may help middle/late chunks adapt more aggressively.

hypothesis: Move batch_phase1_frac from 0.28 to 0.22: creates a 6%-window (22–28% of training) where full batch runs at peak LR before warmdown starts, separating the exploration phase (small-batch high-variance) from a consolidation phase (full-batch peak-LR) before the long decay.
notes: The batch switch (0.28), warmdown start (0.28), and shard reshuffle (0.28) currently all coincide. Moving the batch switch earlier to 0.22 creates a 6% (~36s) window of full-batch training at peak LR. Both compiled batch-size graphs (393k, 786k) are already pre-compiled in the warmup loop — no recompile needed. Exp_134 tried extending phase1 to 0.34 (small batch INTO warmdown) and hurt; this goes the other direction (shorter small-batch, earlier consolidation).

hypothesis: Lower embed_wd further from 0.05 to 0.0: exp_157 confirmed lower embed WD improves val_bpb; with WD=0 the embedding is governed purely by gradient signal (dominated by output projection), consistent with scalar_wd=0.0 already working fine; the output projection gradient is strong enough to self-regularize without explicit WD shrinkage.
notes: Following up on exp_157 (embed_wd 0.085->0.05, keep, -0.0005). The direction is clear: lower embed WD helps. Testing the extreme of WD=0. Risk: without WD, embedding magnitude is unconstrained, but logit_softcap=30 limits effective logit range and int8 quantization with clip_sigmas=20 should handle reasonable embedding growth.

hypothesis: Add 8 learnable frequency-bin logit calibration scalars (zero-init) applied post-softcap: BPE token IDs roughly correlate with subword frequency, so per-bin biases let the model explicitly calibrate its output logit distribution by token frequency class without any new weight matrices.
notes: vocab_bin_ids buffer maps each of 8192 token IDs to one of 8 frequency bins (bin = token_id // 1024, clamped). freq_logit_bias (8 float32, zero-init) is indexed by bin and added to logits after tanh softcap. Added to optimizer_scalar with scalar_lr=0.02. Size impact negligible: 8 floats + 8KB int32 buffer, both near-trivially compressible. Previous tok attempts all modified input embedding (exp_131 per-feature scale, exp_141 BPE position embed, exp_152 smear_alpha) — this is the first to touch the output logit calibration side.

hypothesis: Extend EMA decay from 0.9965→0.9985 (effective window ~286→667 steps): exp_085 showed shortening the window (0.9965→0.99, ~100 steps) badly hurt (Δ=+0.003), implying the optimal window is longer than current; at 10-15 steps/sec on 8 GPUs the flat LR-floor tail spans ~430-650 steps, so the current 286-step window only covers ~50-65% of it—extending to 667 steps achieves full flat-tail averaging for more uniform weight consolidation at convergence.
notes: EMA in the other direction from exp_085 (which went shorter and was very bad). Untried direction. Qualitative threshold: from partial to full flat-tail coverage. Size impact: zero. Single-value change.

hypothesis: Soft sequential injection in parallel-residual layers (7-10): add per-channel learnable parameter parallel_attn_inject [dim=512] to each parallel block, additively injecting attn_out into the MLP input (mlp_in = x_in + inject * attn_out, then mlp_norm). Init=0 → step-0 numerically identical to pure parallel residual; non-zero values smoothly interpolate toward sequential pre-norm where MLP attends to attn output. 4 layers × 512 fp32 = 8 KB raw → float16 passthrough ≈ 4 KB; brotli further. Untried structural DOF — opens up inter-sublayer information flow that exp_137's blanket XSA-disable on parallel layers couldn't access.
notes: The current parallel-residual block has attn and mlp both reading x_in independently — they cannot share information until they merge into the residual. Sequential pre-norm has mlp seeing attn_out via the chained residual. This edit gives parallel blocks a learnable per-channel "soft sequential" mode while preserving parallel additive output structure for stability. Step-0 identity means training proceeds identically to baseline until the optimizer finds non-zero injection values. Distinct from prior parallel-related arch trials: exp_106 (Peri-LN sandwich norm) and exp_137 (disable XSA on parallel) both targeted output-side modifications and failed/overran; this opens an INPUT-side DOF.

hypothesis: Double TTT chunk size from 32K to 64K tokens: same total TTT compute (half as many gradient steps, each with 2× the sequences per rank), but more coherent per-chunk gradient signal and half the chunk-level AllReduce overhead. Hypothesis: fewer but larger-batch gradient steps improve TTT convergence vs noisier 16-seq steps at 32K.
notes: ttt_chunk_tokens: 32768 → 65536. With val_tokens≈2M, this gives ~30 chunks (vs ~60). Each chunk: 32 seqs total, 4 per rank. Per step compute doubles but steps halve → same wall time. No other changes. Rebased from exp_157 (current best, no 0.3 LR floor).

hypothesis: Raise AdamW BETA1 from 0.9 to 0.95 — the only major AdamW hyperparameter untouched in 161 trials. The KellerJordan/modded-nanogpt 320-experiment sweep at d20 found x0_beta1=0.96 optimal vs the legacy 0.9 default; we adopt a conservative 0.95 step toward that finding. Mechanism: longer momentum half-life (10→20 steps) better averages the asymmetric gradient pattern on tied tok_emb (sparse rare-token input grads + dense output-projection grads), and smooths the bifurcated q_gain[H,2] / scalar group whose per-batch gradients fluctuate with token mix; the recently-kept embed_wd reduction (0.085→0.05, exp_157) reduced one source of effective regularization on tok_emb, so additional momentum-side smoothing fills the gap without raising the LR axis itself.
notes: Pure scalar change in the BETA1 default at line 158, zero size/time impact. Affects all three AdamW groups (tok_emb, scalar, head=None dead). The 320-trial modded-nanoGPT sweep is at d=1280 vs our d=512, so the 0.96 optimum may not transfer exactly; 0.95 is a conservative step. Risk: if rare-token sparse grads need MORE responsiveness (not more smoothing), this regresses; would then suggest LOWER beta1 next.

hypothesis: Replace fixed sigma-based row scale (12.85*std/63) with per-row MSE-optimal percentile selection: try 5 clip percentiles [0.999, 0.9995, 0.9999, 0.99999, 1.0] per row and pick the one minimizing reconstruction MSE, giving ~4x finer quantization grid for the bulk of weights vs current near-no-clipping approach.
notes: Inspired by OpenAI's GPTQ-lite (2026-03-22 parameter golf record) which uses percentile-based scale search. Current 12.85*sigma gives essentially no clipping (quantile~1.0), which wastes bit precision. At perc=0.999 the quantization step shrinks ~4x, greatly reducing MSE for on-distribution weights. Works for both matrix (int6, clip_range=63) and embed (int8, clip_range=127) quantization. The clip_sigmas parameter is now effectively unused but kept for API compatibility.

hypothesis: Add per-dimension causal bigram mixing (bigram_mix=[model_dim=512], zero-init): shifts embeddings right-by-1 and element-wise mixes the previous token's embedding into the current before RMSNorm, enriching x0 with bigram context that propagates through all 4 decoder layers via resid_mix injection — near-zero artifact cost (~1KB passthrough float16).
notes: bigram_mix is [D=512] zero-init → identity at step 0, grows gradually during training. Gradients flow both from the current position and as context for the next position, creating a dual-role signal for the embedding. x0 enriched with bigram info reaches every decoder block (layers 7-10) via resid_mix. Added to scalar_params optimizer (scalar_lr=0.02, adam_wd=0.02). numel=512 ≤ 65536 → GPTQ passthrough as float16 (~1KB artifact overhead). | torch.distributed.elastic.multiprocessing.errors.ChildFailedError: ============================================================ (train_gpt.py:77 in flash_attn_3_func)

hypothesis: Replace failed TOP hinge loss with z-loss (α=1e-4 × mean(log²Σexp(logits))): removes exp_161's harmful auxiliary and adds PaLM/T5-style partition-function regularization that sharpens predictions throughout training without any scheduling.
notes: Z-loss penalizes large log-partition-function values, encouraging the model to concentrate probability mass on fewer tokens. With logit_softcap=30.0 and V=8192, log(Z)≈9 at init so z_loss≈0.0081 (0.09% of main CE ~9 nats) — negligible but persistent. As model improves, sharper distributions reduce log(Z) naturally. The coefficient 1e-4 matches PaLM (Chowdhery et al. 2022). Removes all lines from failed exp_161 TOP loss and replaces with 2-line z-loss.

hypothesis: Stochastic depth with linear depth scaling (max_rate=0.1): layer i drops its full residual delta with prob i/10 × 0.1, forcing each layer to independently develop useful representations rather than co-adapting with neighbors; scaled by 1/keep_prob to preserve expected residual magnitude during training.
notes: Rebased to exp_157 (embed_wd=0.05). DropPath applied to (x_out - x_in) delta after full block computation, per-sample Bernoulli mask, inverse-probability scaling. Layer 0: 0% drop; layer 10: 10% drop. No new parameters. Recurrence layers 3-5 each independently sample a mask per loop iteration.

hypothesis: Quarter-batch phase 1 (196k tokens, 12 seqs/GPU) for 0–28% of training: same 2-shape compilation pattern as exp_109 (no 3-shape crash risk), but delivers 4× more optimizer steps at peak LR vs full batch instead of 2×, maximizing exploration before warmdown convergence.
notes: exp_109 (half-batch) gave -0.001645; mechanism appears to be more optimizer steps at high LR. Quarter-batch doubles the step count again (4x vs baseline) while staying within the safe 2-shape compilation window. 3-phase attempts crashed due to 3 compiled shapes; this stays at 2. Potential risk: 12 seqs/GPU may be too noisy for Muon's gradient orthogonalization.

hypothesis: Replace standard CE with focal loss (γ=2) in TTT inner training loop: focal_w=(1-p_t)^2 up-weights hard tokens (domain-specific vocabulary the model currently mispredicts) and suppresses easy tokens, concentrating adaptation signal where it matters most.
notes: Focal loss for TTT: the TTT training objective changes from standard CE to FL(p_t)=-(1-p_t)^2*log(p_t). The focal weight is treated as constant (computed under no_grad), so the backward pass only flows through log_softmax → correct token log-prob. Hard tokens (domain-specific jargon, rare words, unusual constructions) have low p_t and receive near-unit focal weight, driving most of the gradient. Common tokens already predicted near-perfectly have focal_w≈0. This better aligns TTT adaptation with where the model actually needs improvement: domain-specific patterns in the validation data rather than common tokens it already handles well. Zero eval-time cost (same computation structure). Uses forward_logits() for the forward pass instead of model(x,y) to allow custom loss computation.

hypothesis: Muon momentum cooldown 0.99→0.95 after warmup: once the momentum warmup completes at 33% of training, linearly decay Muon momentum from 0.99 to 0.95 over the remaining 67%, reducing momentum inertia during the convergence phase so NS-orthogonalized updates track local gradients more precisely during warmdown.
notes: Momentum cooldown is a genuinely untried axis in 17 opt experiments. All previous schedule changes (cosine, sqrt, sqrt warmdown shapes, EMA window, warmdown timing, β₂ changes) left the Muon momentum fixed at 0.99 post-warmup. The 72% warmdown phase trains with consistently high Muon momentum (high inertia) while LR is falling — decaying momentum alongside LR follows the WSD literature insight that the convergence phase benefits from more local gradient responsiveness. muon_momentum_end=0.95 chosen as a moderate target (above warmup_start=0.92, meaningfully below peak 0.99).

hypothesis: RWKV-V5+ token shift on attention input: per-channel learnable lerp between RMSNormed activations and their 1-step causal shift, applied in every Block. mu init=0 → step-0 identical; gives each layer absolute prev-token info as a structural complement to RoPE's relative-position bias on Q/K, and looped layers 3-5 apply the shift 3× per forward on an evolving residual to expand effective receptive field at no extra compute.
notes: Adds nn.Parameter token_shift_mu [dim=512] per Block (11 total = 5632 fp32 → fp16 passthrough = 11 KB raw, ~3-6 KB brotli). Forward: x_attn_in = x_normed + mu * (F.pad(x_normed[:,:-1,:], (0,0,1,0)) - x_normed). All ops are static-shape elementwise/F.pad → torch.compile fullgraph safe; FA3 unaffected (only its input changes). Untried in arch history (closest precedents — exp_167 sequential injection, exp_106 PeriLN, exp_146 head gate — all add params at the wrong axis: between sublayers or on outputs; this acts on the input axis, mixing across the time dimension). Risk: minor compute add (~0.5% of one block's ops), but no kernel call so should not push eval over budget like exp_106/088.

hypothesis: Add per-dimension causal bigram mixing (bigram_mix=[512], zero-init): uses F.pad to shift embeddings right-by-1, then adds bigram_mix.to(dtype) * prev before RMSNorm, enriching x0 with bigram context that propagates through all decoder layers via resid_mix — ~1KB artifact cost.
notes: Fixed crash from exp_171: prev version used torch.cat + bare bigram_mix*prev which failed with dtype mismatch (float32 param vs bfloat16 activations under autocast). Now uses F.pad(x[:,:-1], (0,0,1,0)) for compile-friendlier causal shift and bigram_mix.to(dtype=x.dtype) to match activation dtype. Pattern mirrors how resid_mix uses .to(dtype=x.dtype) in Block.forward.

hypothesis: Lower enable_looping_at 0.35→0.28 to align loop activation with the batch-switch transition: an UNTOUCHED meta axis in 170 trials. Currently the 7% gap (0.28–0.35) trains a "full-batch + no-looping" graph that is NEVER used at eval (eval uses looped graph 3), so those steps train a config the model is never deployed in. Aligning eliminates the wasteful intermediate phase, giving 7% more wallclock (~42s) to eval-config training. Both transitions now happen on the same step but graph 3 (full-batch+looping) is already precompiled in warmup so there is zero recompile cost. Mechanically supported by Sparse Growing Transformer (arxiv 2603.23998) "progressively extends recurrence" framing — the deep variant gets more time to converge in the regime it will be evaluated in.
notes: Pure constant change line 141: ENABLE_LOOPING_AT default 0.35→0.28. Zero size impact, zero per-step compute change. Constraint check: enable_looping_at >= batch_phase1_frac (0.28) holds (now exact equality), so no new graph shape is introduced at runtime. Warmup still precompiles all three graphs (small-batch+no-loop, full-batch+no-loop, full-batch+loop); graph 2 simply becomes unused at runtime. Risk: minimal — same edge-case as exp_134 noted ("looping always activates with the pre-compiled full-batch shape").

hypothesis: Switch TTT optimizer from SGD+momentum to AdamW(betas=(0.0,0.9), weight_decay=0): beta1=0 eliminates stale cross-chunk momentum while beta2=0.9 builds per-parameter variance estimates across chunks, giving SignSGD-like normalization in early chunk steps — handles heterogeneous quantization error across layers better than flat SGD LR.
notes: Current SGD with momentum=0.9 accumulates gradient direction across chunks; with only 3 epochs/chunk and 30 chunks, the cross-chunk momentum may introduce stale direction from prior different-content chunks. AdamW with beta1=0 makes each chunk's gradient step purely based on current chunk's gradient (no momentum carryover), while beta2=0.9 accumulates a per-parameter variance estimate that normalizes for quantization-induced gradient magnitude differences across layers. The cosine LR schedule and NLL-adaptive scaling remain unchanged. In early steps of each chunk, step ≈ lr * sign(grad) (SignSGD behavior), providing equal normalized adaptation to all parameters regardless of quantization error scale.

hypothesis: Remove exp_172's harmful z-loss and add label smoothing ε=0.02: z-loss at 1e-4 penalizes the log-partition function regardless of correctness (exp_127, exp_172 both hurt), while label_smoothing=0.02 only softens the hard-target gradient by 2% — a clean test from exp_157's baseline since exp_104's ε=0.01 result was confounded by simultaneously reverting a catastrophic per-token clamp.
notes: Current workdir inherited z-loss from exp_172 (discard, +0.003 BPB). Edit: replace z-loss with F.cross_entropy(..., label_smoothing=0.02). With V=8192, each non-target token gets prob += 0.02/8192 ≈ 0.0000024, a very mild softening. Mechanistically different from z-loss: z-loss pushes ALL logit magnitudes down, label smoothing only reduces the correct-class gradient by (1-0.02)=0.98x while adding tiny signal to wrong classes. Risk: if same entropy-increasing mechanism as z-loss → discard ~1.075-1.076; if neutral → matches exp_157 ~1.074207.

hypothesis: MSE-optimal percentile scale selection (exp_170 approach) for all layers, plus int5 for MLP down-projection layers: keeps the ~0.008 BPB quality gain from finer quantization grids on attention/mlp-fc (int6-percentile) while switching mlp.proj to int5-percentile (still 2.5× finer than original sigma=12.85 scheme) to reduce brotli artifact size and clear the 16 MB cap.
notes: exp_170 had the same percentile code but was size_blocked because quantized values span the full [-31,31] range uniformly (higher entropy → worse brotli). Switching mlp.proj to int5 (values in [-15,15], 31 distinct levels) recovers brotli headroom. Quality loss on proj layers is partial: int5+percentile scale ≈ 0.25σ/15 per step, still 2.5× finer than original sigma=12.85 (0.25σ/31 ratio). Expected: better than exp_157 (1.074207) but worse than exp_170 (1.066262 size_blocked).

hypothesis: Split zero-init output projections (attn.proj + mlp.proj) into a dedicated Muon group with muon_wd_proj=0.05, down from 0.095/0.115: zero-init params must grow from zero during training and high WD continuously resists that growth — same mechanism as exp_157's embed_wd reduction for tiny-init embeddings.
notes: attn.proj and mlp.proj both have _zero_init=True. Currently lumped with orthogonally-initialized QKV (WD=0.095) and fc (WD=0.115). New muon_wd_proj=0.05 matches the winning embed_wd level. No change to architecture, LR, or other groups. Increases Muon param groups from 2 to 4.

hypothesis: Muon momentum cooldown 0.99→0.90 (deeper): extend exp_176's winning cooldown endpoint from 0.95 to 0.90, reducing Muon's effective window from ~20 to ~10 steps by end of training for even more precise gradient tracking during late warmdown convergence.
notes: exp_176 showed 0.99→0.95 cooldown gives Δ=-0.001065 (keep). Testing whether deeper cooldown to 0.90 further helps. Mechanism: at β=0.90 the momentum buffer has a ~10-step effective window, making Muon nearly gradient-descent-like at end of training. In Muon, NS orthogonalization already provides noise reduction, so low momentum mainly affects direction consistency vs. responsiveness tradeoff. Risk: too-low momentum destabilizes final convergence.

hypothesis: Raise AdamW BETA2 from 0.95 to 0.98 — untouched in 179 trials (only beta1 tested at exp_169, adam_eps at exp_135). Current beta1=beta2=0.95 collapses m and v EMA half-lives to ~14 steps, defeating Adam's variance-adaptation purpose; restoring m << v separation (v half-life ~35 steps) gives more stable per-row effective LR on tied tok_emb (8192×512) and on the AdamW scalar group (q_gain[H,2], attn_scale, mlp_scale, resid_mix, skip_gates) especially across the 72% warmdown. Backed by the Fantastic Pretraining Optimizers paper (arxiv 2509.02046) using beta2=0.98 as 130M baseline.
notes: Single-character default change (0.95 → 0.98). Zero size impact, zero per-step compute change. Affects all three AdamW optimizer groups (optimizer_tok, optimizer_scalar, plus per-layer scalar group). Token-half-life perspective: at 786k tokens/step, β₂=0.95 gives ~11M token half-life vs β₂=0.98 gives ~27M tokens, the latter aligning with canonical recipe proportions (5-10% of total ~550M training tokens). Distinct from exp_135 (lower eps amplifies v sensitivity) and exp_169 (beta1 tweak) — those adjacent axes were tested independently.

hypothesis: V-RMSNorm symmetric to existing Q/K norm (HybridNorm 2503.04598, 2025): add F.rms_norm to V after c_v projection so QKV are all unit-norm per head. Parameter-free, single extra rms_norm over Hkv=4 vs Q's Hk=8.
notes: Single-line edit; zero new params; eval cost about half a Q-norm; well below the 2-norm-per-block budget that tripped exp_106. Backed by HybridNorm 2025 ablations.

hypothesis: Add learnable output_shift [model_dim=512] zero-init, applied to x before the logit projection: F.linear(x+shift, W) = raw_logits + W@shift, giving a per-token logit bias via the embedding rows at only 512 params (2KB) to learn frequency priors explicitly without the 32KB cost of [vocab_size] bias.
notes: Motivation: tied embeddings must serve dual input/output roles; an explicit [dim]-dim output shift provides a fast-converging frequency calibration channel that decouples the semantic and frequency-prior learning in the embedding matrix. At zero-init, step-0 is numerically identical to baseline. Gradient for output_shift = W.T @ grad_logits, which is the mean embedding-weighted prediction error — converges quickly to frequency corrections. Size-safe: only adds 2KB before brotli vs 8.5KB headroom from exp_176 (15991464 bytes).

hypothesis: Stratified shard sampling: replace proportional-remaining shard selection with round-robin via shuffled shard permutation each batch step, guaranteeing all shards contribute equally to every batch (vs proportional which statistically skips ~12% of shards per step with 12 shards and 24 seqs).
notes: The old-stack domain notes say Latin-square/stratified kept TWICE. Proportional sampling with n_shards=12 and batch_size=24 gives each shard ~2.0±1.3 seqs per step with ~12% chance of any shard being unsampled. Round-robin forces exactly 2 seqs per shard per step. The shuffled shard_perm is regenerated each batch call so there is no fixed ordering artifact. Fallback to proportional if a shard is exhausted (effectively dead code in 600s training where no shard is fully consumed). Also improves GPTQ calibration diversity since calibration also calls next_batch.

hypothesis: Add SLOT-style logit-bias correction to TTT scoring: a [vocab_size=8192] float32 bias is zero-initialized, analytically updated each chunk via the CE gradient (model_pred_freq - target_freq, LR=0.5) using already-computed forward-pass logits (no extra backward), then applied at scoring of subsequent chunks — captures token-frequency distribution shift complementary to weight-level TTT adaptation.
notes: Logit-bias SLOT-style correction added purely at scoring time. The analytical gradient (softmax - one_hot_avg) is computed from already-computed scoring logits, costing ~0.5s extra eval. Logit bias carries over across chunks (no reset), accumulating a global frequency correction as val proceeds. Adaptation loop unchanged.

hypothesis: Int4+percentile for all matrix layers: use MSE-optimal percentile scale selection (exp_170 approach) with int4 quantization (clip_range=7, values in [-7,7]) instead of int6+sigma. Int4+percentile step size ≈ 2.5σ/7 = 0.36σ is marginally finer than int6+sigma (12.85σ/31 = 0.41σ), so quality should match or beat baseline, while 15 distinct values vs 63 allows brotli to compress the artifact back under 16 MB.
notes: exp_170 (int6+percentile all layers) was 23.5 MB, 7.5 MB over limit. exp_182 (int6/int5 mix) also 23.5 MB. The root cause: percentile scales make values span full range uniformly so brotli achieves near-0 compression. Int4 reduces entropy to ~3.9 bits/value, targeting ~11-13 MB artifact. Quality tradeoff: int4+percentile step=0.36σ vs int6+sigma step=0.41σ — essentially same reconstruction quality but now fits the artifact budget.

hypothesis: Raise ttt_lr from 0.005 to 0.008: with AdamW(beta1=0) the effective per-step update is ~lr×sign(gradient), so total weight movement per chunk = 3 steps × lr; at 0.005 the model moves only 1.5% of typical weight magnitude, likely underfitting the adaptation budget — increasing to 0.008 gives 2.4% movement while keeping max effective LR (with nll_scale=2.0 and cosine=1.0) at 0.016, still conservative.
notes: ttt_lr is the only major TTT hyperparameter that has never been changed in 30+ eval experiments. The Test-Time Training Done Right paper (2505.23884) recommends aggressive optimizers for TTT; our conservative lr=0.005 may be leaving adaptation capacity on the table. With AdamW(beta1=0,beta2=0.9), sign-SGD-like step size = lr so effective max movement is small; 60% increase should improve chunk adaptation without budget risk (same number of steps).

hypothesis: Combined freq+loss GPTQ Hessian weighting: multiply existing per-token loss weights (exp_130) by per-token frequency weights sqrt(freq[x_t]/mean_freq), biasing Hessian accumulation toward tokens that are both uncertain (high loss) AND frequent (high BPB impact), targeting the intersection of the two previously separate gains.
notes: exp_002 (freq-only weighting) gave -0.0018 vs old baseline. exp_130 (loss-only) gave -0.00005 marginal additional. Combined product (loss × freq) captures tokens important on BOTH axes simultaneously. Sigma-based scales restored (percentile approach is size_blocked at 23.5 MB regardless of bit-width). Novel combination not previously attempted in this lineage. | torch.distributed.elastic.multiprocessing.errors.ChildFailedError: ============================================================ (train_gpt.py:971 in collect_hessians)

hypothesis: Delay Muon momentum cooldown start from frac=0.33 to frac=0.70: hold momentum at peak 0.99 during the majority of warmdown (33-70%), then do a faster 0.99→0.95 cooldown only in the final 30%, letting high momentum escape poor basins despite declining LR before switching to precise gradient tracking at convergence.
notes: Three-phase momentum schedule: (1) warmup 0.92→0.99 over 0-33%, (2) stable at 0.99 from 33-70%, (3) cooldown 0.99→0.95 from 70-100%. Current code (exp_176) starts cooldown immediately at warmup-end (33%), slowly reaching 0.95 by 100%. The late-cooldown version keeps momentum high during early warmdown where LR is still substantial, only reducing momentum in the final convergence phase. muon_momentum_cooldown_start_frac=0.70 added as new hyperparameter.

hypothesis: Lower muon_wd_mlp from 0.115 to 0.095 to equalize MLP and attention weight decay: in the 10-minute undertrained regime MLP is capacity-constrained by time not overfitting, and its 5.2x shrinkage-to-LR ratio vs attn's 4.3x unnecessarily limits MLP expressiveness; also isolating the WD split that was conflated with ttt_epochs change in exp_013.
notes: muon_wd_mlp: 0.115 → 0.095. Rebased from exp_176. exp_013 introduced muon_wd_mlp=0.115 alongside ttt_epochs 3→2 change simultaneously, so the MLP WD split was never cleanly isolated. Equalization hypothesis: both matrix groups should have same WD since there's no evidence of differential overfitting in undertrained regime.

hypothesis: Byte-weighted CE training loss: weight each token's NLL by its actual encoded byte count (base_bytes + leading_space from SP LUTs), normalized per batch — directly aligning the training CE objective with BPB = sum(NLL_t)/sum(bytes_t), so the optimizer prioritizes multi-byte subwords (like ' the'=4B, 'tion'=4B) over single-byte tokens (',','a') proportionally to their BPB contribution.
notes: All previous loss modifications were harmful. Byte-weighting is the only theoretically motivated approach: BPB = sum(NLL)/sum(bytes), so byte-weighted CE is exactly BPB. Uses existing build_sentencepiece_luts to get per-token byte counts (_bw_base: int16, _bw_space: bool) as non-persistent buffers. Per-batch normalization keeps loss scale stable. Fullgraph-compile-safe ops only.

hypothesis: Raise muon_momentum_warmup_start 0.92→0.95: untouched higher-side bracket of an axis where exp_125 already showed lower (0.85) hurts. With current batch curriculum (half-size phase 1, frac 0–0.28) the small-batch noisy gradient phase coincides exactly with the lowest Muon momentum during warmup ramp — raising the start narrows ramp range from 0.92→0.99 (7pp) to 0.95→0.99 (3pp), increasing gradient averaging during the noisier small-batch phase without delaying the warmup peak; cooldown logic (start=warmup-end at frac=0.33, end=0.95) is unchanged so late-training dynamics are identical.
notes: Single-character default change (0.92→0.95) on line 154. Zero size, zero per-step compute. Affects only the first 33% of training (warmup ramp). Mechanism: at frac=0.165 (mid-warmup) momentum becomes 0.97 vs 0.955 prior — more averaging during half-batch noise. Synergy with exp_109 batch curriculum: the half-batch phase ends at frac=0.28, very close to warmup_fraction=0.33, so the entire small-batch phase runs at higher-momentum smoothing. Literature support: arxiv 2509.01440 notes "at higher momentum values some optimizers no longer benefit from warmup" — effectively shortens the ramp by starting closer to peak. exp_125 (0.85, deeper) failed +0.0027; this samples the symmetric shallower direction.

hypothesis: Replace additive output_shift (exp_187, +0.000170 BPB penalty) with multiplicative output_scale [model_dim=512] ones-init: x_scaled = x * output_scale before the tied embedding projection, giving a learned per-dimension diagonal reweighting of logit similarities that decouples which embedding dimensions matter for prediction vs. input lookup — exp_089 tried this technique but hit eval_budget_overrun without producing a valid bpb; now retried on the current (eval-stable) codebase.
notes: Removes output_shift (zero-init [512] additive, discarded in exp_187 at +0.000170). Replaces with output_scale (ones-init [512] multiplicative). Both route through scalar optimizer (scalar_wd=0.0, scalar_lr=0.02). The multiplicative scale learns a diagonal metric in the output projection space — equivalent to making the final-norm output have a learnable per-dim weight before projection. This is conceptually what exp_089 tried (eval_budget_overrun on a different lineage before the eval timing was fixed), so it deserves a clean test.

hypothesis: test

Mask first 64 positions from training loss to align gradient with stride-64 sliding-window eval: positions 0–63 per sequence have no prior context (cold-start) while every evaluated token (after chunk 0) has ≥64 tokens of context; slicing logits/targets at [:, 64:] is a static shape change compile-safe.
notes: exp_022 tried position-weighting (ramp 0.5→1.5) and got eval_budget_overrun with F.cross_entropy(weight=...). This approach simply slices—cheaper than baseline, no budget risk. TTT inner loop also benefits from masking cold-start positions since val evaluation never scores position<context_size (=1984) except in the first window.

hypothesis: Combined freq+loss GPTQ Hessian weighting: multiply existing per-token loss weights by per-token frequency weights sqrt(freq[x_t]/mean_freq) — fix GPU→CPU bug from exp_192 crash, same mathematical intent: bias Hessian toward tokens that are both uncertain (high loss) AND frequent (high BPB impact).
notes: exp_192 crashed because calib_batches contains GPU tensors — torch.bincount needs CPU input and freq_scale indexing needs CPU indices. Fixed with .cpu() calls. Same hypothesis as exp_192: combined loss*freq weighting.
